# Chapter 1 — The Bottom of the List

**One-line summary:** Holroyd arrives in Cusco, meets Isobel and Mariano for the first time as a party, learns he is twelve days behind Riedel, and makes his first act of trust — deferring to Mariano's route correction without making it a ceremony.

**POV:** James Holroyd — third-person limited, past tense.

**Setting / Time:** Cusco market district; a tienda near the market; the road south toward the Yanatile valley head. Late August 1938, pre-dawn arrival through early afternoon departure.

---

## Goal

Assemble the party, complete the provisioning, and depart for the Yanatile valley the same day — before the gap with Riedel widens further.

## Obstacle

Holroyd has hired Isobel and Mariano by correspondence only; he is assessing them against the expedition's demands in real time, while they are doing the same to him. The party must reach functional trust in the hours before departure. Holroyd's habit of authority — the Survey of India reflex — is wrong for this team and he will have to revise it before the morning is out.

---

## Action Beats

- Holroyd steps off the overnight train from Lima onto the Cusco platform before dawn, carrying a cartridge case of survey instruments and the Leica in his jacket inner pocket. He has been fourth on Finch's list. He is aware of this. He does not find it humiliating; he finds it accurate. Brief interiority: the cable from Finch, the fee (enough for three months of debts in Lima), the only facts he was given — that two other men declined and one died. He is here because the eastern Cordillera is his range and he has nothing better to lose.

- He walks to the market by the San Pedro arcade to collect the last of the food stores he arranged from Lima: hardtack biscuits, dried chuño, canned sardines packed in oil, coca tea. He is counting 80-lb atadito equivalents in his head — standard load discipline — when Isobel appears.

- First exchange with Isobel: she has already been at the provisions depot for forty minutes and she has a problem. The coca leaf order is short by a significant margin. She has corrected it, she says — added sixteen pounds from a market vendor she trusts — and she will want the cost reimbursed. Holroyd asks her to explain the coca calculation. She does: at altitude above 15,000 feet, the men function or they stop functioning, and the difference is sometimes sixteen pounds of coca leaf. Holroyd: "How much did you spend?" She tells him. He writes it in his notebook and says: "Fine." She registers that he didn't argue the logic; she files this.

- Mariano arrives at the market with four mules and the two arrieros — Cleto and Rufino — from his Yanatile valley. The mules are better-conditioned than Holroyd expected. Mariano is relashing a load on the second mule and does not look up when Holroyd says his name. He finishes the lashing, checks the knot, then looks up. Holroyd: "Quispe." Mariano: "Holroyd." They shake hands. Holroyd's surveyor's eye: the man is built for uphill work, carries nothing extra, moves without waste. The mules respond to him before he speaks.

- Cleto and Rufino are introduced briefly — they will work the lower approach and turn back at the snowline. Holroyd observes that Rufino is younger than expected. He will not know this observation matters until the gorge in Chapter 2.

- Over a meal at the tienda — sardines, bread, coca tea, no alcohol: Holroyd's rule — Isobel receives a note passed in by a trader who came through the Yanatile valley head two days ago. A German party of five crossed the trailhead sixteen days ago, heavily loaded, well-equipped. Holroyd calculates: he expected to have a week's head start. Instead he is twelve days behind. The race he has been treating as theoretical becomes actual.

- He spreads his 1934 survey maps on the table. His planned approach: east up the Yanatile main valley, north branch at the Pampa Chica junction, southeast ascent to SM-7 through the high puna. He shows the line to Mariano.

- Mariano studies the maps in silence. Holroyd has seen men perform map-reading for an audience; this is not that. Mariano's eyes move across the lines methodically, tracing contours. Then his finger traces a different route — north of the valley head, cutting across the upper puna three miles further, approaching SM-7 from the north-northeast.

- Holroyd: "That adds two days." Mariano: "One. The dry makes the upper puna fast. The southeast approach loses a mule above thirteen thousand, guaranteed. The south face has a gorge section that breaks loads." He pauses. "I lost one there in 1929." Another pause. "You will too."

- Holroyd looks at Isobel across the table. She says: "He's right about the south gorge. Three people I trust have said the same thing." Holroyd looks back at the map. Mariano has not moved his finger. The evidence is the evidence.

- Holroyd takes out his pencil and marks the new line on the map. He does not make a production of it. He says: "We leave at first light tomorrow — which means packs ready tonight." He folds the map. Mariano, for the first time, looks faintly satisfied — not smug; satisfied, the way a man looks when a load is balanced correctly.

- Evening: Holroyd in his room above the tienda, writing in his notebook. He writes the revised route, the supply inventory, the atadito distribution. He writes: "Riedel: 12 days ahead. Approx. 5 men and full Ahnenerbe equipment budget." He writes nothing about the route correction. The facts of it are written in the map. He does not need to annotate the fact that Mariano knew better.

- He pulls out the Leica, checks the shutter speed in the lamplight. It clicks correctly at all settings. He reloads it from the film canister in his jacket pocket: 36 exposures, Kodak Super-X panchromatic. He puts it back against his chest, inside the jacket.

---

## Outcome / Turn

The party is assembled and provisioned; the route is corrected. Holroyd has already revised his authority downward once — quietly, correctly — and the team's functioning dynamic is established. They depart at first light. But they are twelve days behind, and the gap may be worse than it looks: Riedel's team has been in the field longer and is therefore better acclimatized to altitude. Their speed on the puna may be higher than Holroyd's estimates.

---

## New Question or Pressure Raised

The German party had a full Ahnenerbe equipment budget — more porters, better rope, more food. What does twelve days of head start mean on the mountain itself? Is Riedel at the puna already, or above the snowline? And why did none of the three men Finch hired before Holroyd take this job?

---

## Continuity Notes (New Facts Introduced)

- Holroyd's Leica IIIa is carried against his chest inside his outer jacket, loaded with 36-exposure Kodak Super-X panchromatic film.
- Four mules are part of the expedition; Mariano manages them.
- Cleto and Rufino are hired by Mariano from the Yanatile valley; they will work only the lower approach.
- Holroyd carries his .30-06 sporting rifle, acquired in La Paz in 1935, in the kit.
- The route to SM-7 is revised from the southeast approach to a north-northeast approach, on Mariano's recommendation.
- Riedel's party of five departed the Yanatile trailhead sixteen days before Holroyd's party.
- The party departs Cusco in late August 1938.

# Chapter 2 — Selva Alta

**One-line summary:** Holroyd overrides Mariano's request for two hours to repair the bridge anchor; Rufino is on the bridge when it fails; the mule and two ataditos of critical supplies are lost; Rufino survives, but Holroyd was wrong, and the party knows it.

**POV:** James Holroyd — third-person limited, past tense.

**Setting / Time:** The selva alta on the eastern slopes, 8,000–10,500 feet. Mule trails through cloud forest; a rope-bridge gorge crossing; a camp at approximately 9,500 feet. Three to four days after Chapter 1.

---

## Goal

Move through the cloud forest as fast as the mules will allow, cross the gorge system intact, and reach the transition to puna before the afternoon cloud comes in or a rain event turns the trails to mud.

## Obstacle

The trail conditions are against speed: single-file paths, nothing on the downhill side, perpetual moisture. The bridges are maintained by local communities on their own schedule, not on any expedition's. And Holroyd is impatient — he is trying to close the twelve-day gap and his impatience is a form of bad arithmetic: haste on a wet rope bridge does not shorten the distance to Riedel; it shortens the margin between functioning and not.

---

## Action Beats

**Trail conditions established:**
- The smell of wet soil, rotting bromeliads, the sound of the stream below. The mule trail is cut by centuries of use and shows it: packed earth worn to the substrate, roots exposed as steps and trip hazards, the edge on the downhill side dropping without railing into cloud and noise. Mariano walks in front. The party moves in single file. The mules manage better than Holroyd on the wet roots — he notes this without pleasure.
- The mist burns off by mid-morning to reveal orchids clamped to mossy bark forty feet up. Holroyd does not pause to look at them. He does notice them, and notice that he does not pause.

**Moving and talking (bodies in motion, not face-to-face):**
- Isobel falls into step beside Holroyd on a wider section of trail. She relays a message from a man in the last village — a Quechua farmer who knew her father and asked her to "tell him what you find." She says this without particular color, the way she delivers all practical information. Holroyd asks: "Your father knew this man?" She says: "He knew a great many people in this valley." A pause. "He died six weeks ago." Holroyd: "I know." He says nothing more. She does not expect him to.
- She translates something useful: the farmer also mentioned a German party that came through two weeks ago with four men and more rope than they should need for a survey expedition. They were asking about the northern pastures and the peaks above.

**The bridge:**
- They reach the gorge in early afternoon. The bridge: two anchor posts sunk in Inca stonework foundations, the rest hemp and rough-cut planking. It spans forty feet across a gorge where the river is thirty-five feet below. The bridge is functional — it takes Holroyd's weight when he steps on the first three planks and shakes the side rope. He runs his eye along the underside. The planking looks sound.
- Mariano walks to the downstream anchor post and crouches. He examines a section of the main load rope where it passes through the iron pin at the post base. He finds what he finds without announcing it: the rope fibers are fraying where they contact the iron, water-weakened, perhaps fifteen percent of the rope's load-bearing cross-section still intact. He stands up and says, in Spanish: "Give me two hours. I can replace this section with the spare hemp from my pack." He holds up the rope he means to replace.
- Holroyd calculates: two hours here, which with the afternoon cloud coming means camping on this side of the gorge, which means losing a full day in the gorge system. The gap with Riedel is already twelve days. He looks at the rope. It is frayed, yes, but the bridge held him. He says: "We don't have two hours." He tells Rufino to take the heaviest mule across first — the one with the tent poles and bulk food stores, the atadito that can't be redistributed easily — and to lead it slowly and don't stop at midspan.

**SET PIECE — the bridge fails:**
- Rufino takes the lead rope and steps onto the bridge. Mariano watches without speaking. The mule follows, three feet behind, its weight at about 160 pounds with two full ataditos: 80 pounds per side, canvas-wrapped, balanced. The first ten feet hold. The planking creaks but does not flex unduly.
- At midspan — twenty feet out — the mule's combined weight and movement load the rope in the way walking loads rope differently from standing: a rhythmic pulse, not a static weight. The frayed section at the downstream anchor begins to unwind. The right-side railing plank pulls loose from its hemp lashing at the first stanchion. The next plank follows.
- Rufino is moving when the right side gives. His left hand closes on the remaining left-side rope as the planking under him opens; he swings out and slams into the gorge wall — left shoulder, both knees taking the impact. He hangs, gripping the rope with both hands, feet scrabbling on the rock face. The mule is not fast enough. It goes with the planking. Two ataditos follow it. The sound: a crack like a rifle shot when the main load rope finally pulls through the iron pin, then the rope whipping, then the canvas hitting the water thirty-five feet below and then nothing but the river.
- Mariano has already moved. He is flat on the gorge edge with the spare rope in his hands before the mule hits the water. He has tied a running loop; he lowers it to Rufino's level. Rufino gets his right forearm through the loop. Mariano hauls. Cleto has Mariano's legs, anchoring him. Holroyd stands at the edge and watches Rufino come up the rock face, clumsy, both hands bleeding, both knees cut through his trousers.

**The aftermath:**
- Rufino sits on the ground. He is shaking. His hands are opening and closing around nothing. Isobel is already kneeling beside him, unrolling the medical kit from her pack.
- Holroyd stands at the gorge edge looking at the water, where the canvas is already gone. What went with the mule: the second primus stove, a week's emergency rations, the complete tent pole set. They still have the oil-canvas tent body but the poles are gone; above the snowline they will rig with alpine ropes and whatever hardwood Mariano can find at the treeline. With only one primus stove, they cannot afford any delay above 16,000 feet — a blocked pass, an extra day of weather, anything.
- Holroyd opens his field notebook. He writes the inventory of what was lost: stove, ration quantity, tent poles. He writes the load redistribution he will need to perform. He writes none of this under a heading that names who made the call. He is aware of not writing it, and aware that this awareness is a form of the same bad accounting that destroyed his Survey career — not dishonesty exactly, but the careful selection of what goes in the record.
- He closes the notebook. He says to the group: "We camp here tonight. Rufino, you're light duty tomorrow." He says to Mariano: "Can you rig the tent without poles?" Mariano: "Yes." Nothing more.
- Evening camp: Mariano disappears for forty minutes and returns with four hardwood branches from the lower treeline, cut to length. He has pre-cut them — or someone has; they are too uniform to be fresh. He doesn't explain this. The tent goes up using the branches and spare alpine rope as a ridge-and-brace system. It holds.
- Holroyd eats his ration and does the arithmetic. Two weeks instead of three before they have to turn back. No margin for delays above the snowline. He looks at Isobel across the small fire. She says: "We're still going." It is not a question. He says: "We're still going."

**The crossing, completed:**
- Next morning, Mariano splices the damaged anchor rope with his spare hemp and crosses first, checking each plank. Then Isobel. Then Holroyd. Then Cleto with the two remaining load mules, unloaded — the loads carried by hand. The crossing takes two hours. It is slower than the bridge repair would have been.

---

## Outcome / Turn

They are across, intact, with three fewer loads and twelve days still behind Riedel. Rufino is functional but slow; his hands will be usable within a week but they're not usable now. The bridge incident has cost them their margin — no delays above the snowline are survivable now against their reduced provisions — and it has established, without being stated, that Holroyd made a wrong call and the party absorbed it. He made no announcement; neither did anyone else. The silence around the fact is its own kind of accounting.

---

## New Question or Pressure Raised

Riedel's party came through the gorge system two weeks ago. Which bridge did they use? If they took the south approach Mariano overruled, they faced a different — possibly worse — gorge section. Are they ahead on the mountain because their equipment is better, or because their route, however different, had its own difficulties? And what does their bridge crossing look like without a Mariano?

---

## Continuity Notes (New Facts Introduced)

- **The second primus stove is lost** — the party now has one primus.
- **Emergency rations are reduced to two weeks** — no delays above 16,000 feet are survivable at the current provision level.
- **The tent poles are lost**; the tent is now rigged with hardwood branch braces and alpine rope, functional but slower to set.
- **Rufino survives** — both hands lacerated, both knees cut; he can walk but is not at full capacity.
- The bridge failure is caused by a fraying anchor rope at the downstream iron pin. Mariano identified it and Holroyd overrode him.
- One mule and two 80-lb ataditos are lost in the gorge river.
- The rope bridge used a hemp load rope through an iron pin in Inca stonework anchor posts — standard regional construction.

# Chapter 3 — Puna

**One-line summary:** On the high puna a herder woman mentions Isobel's father; that night Isobel opens his annotated copy of Arce's account for the first time since he died and discovers his belief in Rititambo was not displacement but a decades-long act of testimony — and her grief, which has been shapeless, begins to find a form.

**POV:** Isobel Carrow — third-person limited, past tense. This is Isobel's chapter. The narration has slightly more interior access than in the Holroyd chapters, following her as she works something through; it stays in observation and inference and does not name feeling in abstract terms.

**Setting / Time:** The upper selva alta transitioning to the open puna; a herder encampment at approximately 13,000 feet. Late August, five days after the gorge crossing.

---

## Goal (Isobel's)

Move the party through the puna herder territory efficiently — read the social landscape correctly, get accurate information about the German party and the route above — and manage her own relationship to why she is here, which has been pressing on her since the village man asked her to tell her father what she finds.

## Obstacle

The herder woman gives information in the Andean oblique — accurate and incomplete, nothing withheld dishonestly, everything dependent on the quality of the question. The German party has been through and asked in the wrong way. Isobel will have to ask correctly, and then translate for Holroyd, and the gap between what the woman says and what Holroyd receives will be visible. The harder obstacle is the oilskin case in Isobel's pack — her father's annotated copy of Arce's account, which she has been carrying since Pisac and has not opened.

---

## Action Beats

**The treeline gives way:**
- The last ferns, the last mossy trunks, the last smell of wet earth. Then the open puna: tussock ichu grass to the horizon, the sky arriving like a door swung wide. The light is different up here — harder, the shadows shorter, ultraviolet beginning to work on the backs of hands. Isobel watches Holroyd's body shift slightly as the terrain opens. He is a man who functions better with a visible horizon. She files this, as she files everything about the people she works with.
- The wind is constant. The ichu bends and rights itself in long, slow waves. There are clouds building to the east over the Amazon basin, the daily convection cycle, but the sky to the west is the high Andean blue — thin and specific and reliable.

**The herder encampment:**
- Two low stone houses, walls thick enough to hide inside the wind shadow. An alpaca pen, stone-walled. Three children playing in the lee of the second house. One woman of perhaps sixty — grey hair, a wool hat pulled low, hands that have been at altitude all her life. The men are with the animals on the upper pastures. The woman offers them muña mint tea. It is very thin at this altitude but it is offered correctly and Isobel accepts correctly, which the woman registers.
- The woman asks, with the question's particular Quechua flatness that means she already knows the answer: where are they going? Isobel says: to the upper snowfields of SM-7. (She uses Holroyd's survey designation and then, on impulse, adds the old description from Arce: the lee face, the sealed place.) The woman is quiet for a moment. Then: the Germans came through nine days ago, she says. Not twelve. Nine. Four of them, heavily equipped, a Peruvian man among them. They asked about the northern pastures.
- Isobel: "What did you tell them?" The woman: "The pastures. Where the water stays in dry season. The snow on the northern face." She pauses. "They asked about the old places above. I told them what I tell those people." Isobel: "What is that?" The woman, with no particular edge: "Nothing worth knowing."
- Isobel translates for Holroyd, standing slightly back: "She confirms nine days. They asked about the old places. She says she didn't tell them anything useful." She does not translate the last phrase exactly — she renders it as: "She says she didn't give them topographical detail." This is accurate enough and leaves out what Holroyd doesn't need and would not know how to receive.

**The woman says something Isobel doesn't translate immediately:**
- The woman looks at Isobel with the particular attention of someone placing a face they've been expecting. She says, in Quechua: "Your father knew the right questions." Isobel goes very still. "He came through here once. Long ago. Asking about the sealed places — but not for himself." She pauses. "He asked in the way people ask when they are carrying a question for someone else."
- Isobel: "You knew him." The woman: "I knew of him. Word comes down the valley. He stayed for three days at the Tacana family's place, asking and writing. He told the right people what he found." She looks at Mariano, briefly, then back at Isobel. "That is a long work. Patient work."
- A silence. Then Isobel: "Did he reach the sealed place?" The woman: "No. But he knew where it was. The knowing was enough for what he needed to do."
- Mariano has been sitting at the edge of the group throughout this exchange. Isobel watches him from the corner of her eye. He has not moved, has not indicated he is following the Quechua, but he is following the Quechua. When the woman speaks of the right people who were told, Mariano's eyes settle briefly on the middle distance — not checked out, not absent. Present in a different way.
- Isobel does not translate this section for Holroyd.

**The camp — Isobel opens the oilskin case:**
- Holroyd has already entered the tent to review the route with Mariano; they are talking in their functional Spanish, mule counts and elevation estimates. The fire outside is low but warm.
- Isobel sits outside the tent with the oilskin case in her hands. She has been carrying it since the day she cleared her father's room in Pisac — six weeks ago, while his body was still cooling two rooms away and the women of the congregation were singing in the courtyard. She has not opened it because it seemed wrong somehow to open it there, and then it seemed too soon, and then the expedition preparation had taken over and the wrongness had become a habit.
- She opens it now. Inside: the friar Arce's 1573 account in a printed copy with a hand-sewn binding — her father's own. The margins are dense with his handwriting, in layers of ink that mark decades of return visits to the same text. Some in English (early, the hand of a young man in his thirties). Some in Spanish (the middle years — he started thinking in Spanish sometime in the 1910s, she knows this). Some in Quechua (the last decade, the hand slightly thicker, the pen held differently after the arthritis started).
- She reads the marginalia in the lamplight. Not Arce's account — she has read that; she knows it — but what her father wrote beside it.
- What she finds: he was cross-referencing Arce's descriptions against testimony he gathered over thirty years from the communities of the Urubamba valley, the Yanatile valley, the Apurímac headwaters. Dates and names in the margins she does not recognize. But the structure is unmistakable: he was building a concordance between what Arce saw and what the valley communities held in oral tradition. He was not trying to confirm his own faith in the accounts' authenticity. He was trying to make a record, for the communities, that connected what the missionaries had written to what they already knew. A kind of restitution — a document that the communities could use as corroboration in their own terms.
- In the last pages of the margin notes, written in the last year or two — she knows the hand, the stiffness — he lists twelve names of people who told him specific things about Rititambo. Beside each name, a location and a date. Beside each entry, the matching passage in Arce's account.
- The last entry: a name she doesn't know, followed by a single Quechua phrase she translates for herself: "the witness who goes and returns and makes it real."
- Isobel has been wrong about what her father was doing. She thought he was retreating from the fact of his life — that he was a man who had given thirty-one years to a community that was genuinely fond of him and would always remain, in some essential way, foreign to him, and that the Rititambo obsession was a way of finding meaning in mystery rather than facing that fact. This reading felt correct to her. It was wrong. He was not retreating; he was participating, in the only way he knew how, in something that was not his and was not about him.
- She closes the book. The grief has been shapeless since he died — she has been carrying it in the way she carries her pack, which means not talking about it but letting it shape the load distribution. Now it begins to have a shape. The shape is not simple. He was doing something she would respect. He did it for thirty years without telling her, because it was not hers to carry — it was for the people of the valley. She can be angry about that (she was the one who thought it was displacement) and it doesn't change what he was doing. She sits with this for a while, in the wind.

**Isobel and Mariano, briefly:**
- Mariano comes out of the tent to check the mule tethers before sleep. He passes close to Isobel. He does not look at the book she is holding — or does not obviously look. She says, in Quechua: "He listed twelve names. The last one says 'the witness who goes and returns.'" Mariano secures a tether. He says, in Quechua: "Your father was careful." He goes back inside. This is the most Mariano will say about it, and it is enough — Isobel understands that "careful" means something specific in this context: he was careful with the knowledge, which means he knew whose knowledge it was.

---

## Outcome / Turn

The gap to Riedel is nine days, not twelve — they are gaining less than they thought, but they are gaining. The herder woman's route information is solid. And Isobel's relationship to the expedition has shifted: she is no longer arriving to confirm or refute her father's belief, which was never really what he was doing. She is arriving to see what he spent thirty years trying to prepare a witness for. This is harder and more specific than her previous motive, and she is not entirely comfortable with it.

---

## New Question or Pressure Raised

The herder woman said Isobel's father told "the right people" what he found, and she looked at Mariano when she said it. Does Isobel understand what that means? She understands enough. Does she tell Holroyd? She does not — not because she is keeping a secret from him but because it is not his question to carry. The question of what Mariano is really doing here is beginning to take shape, and Isobel is letting it take shape rather than extracting it.

---

## Continuity Notes (New Facts Introduced)

- Riedel's party passed through this herder encampment **nine days ago**, not twelve. The gap between the parties has narrowed faster than expected.
- The herder woman confirms Riedel asked about the old places but was given no useful information about them.
- **Reverend Thomas Carrow visited this region** — specifically the herder communities of the Yanatile valley — and spent decades cross-referencing Arce's account with oral testimony, building a community-oriented concordance. The herder woman knew of him.
- Isobel's father compiled **a list of twelve named witnesses** from valley communities who knew of Rititambo, with specific references to Arce's account. This document is in the margins of her copy of the Arce account.
- The herder woman looked at Mariano when she spoke of "the right people" Reverend Carrow told.
- Isobel now understands that her father's Rititambo work was an act of restitution toward the valley communities, not personal displacement.
- Isobel does not translate the full exchange about her father for Holroyd — only the route and timing information.

# Chapter 4 — Snowline

**One-line summary:** Cleto and Rufino turn back with the mules; Mariano leads the three above the snowline alone; he finds Riedel's fire ring at 16,500 feet, still holding warmth — the gap has collapsed from nine days to thirty-six hours, and the rock formation that marks the bowl entrance is visible above.

**POV:** Mariano Quispe — third-person limited, past tense. This is Mariano's chapter. The narration is the most externally observant in the book: less interior than any other chapter, more attention to terrain, weather, and other people's behavior. What Mariano knows is held in the body and in the landscape.

**Setting / Time:** The upper puna transitioning to permanent snowfield; the camp at 15,000 feet; the hard-snow ascent to 16,500 feet. First week of September 1938. The dry season above the snowline: wind, frost, no moisture, no cloud until afternoon.

---

## Goal (Mariano's)

Get the three people above the snowline safely — assess the terrain, maintain a pace that keeps altitude sickness manageable, and do not reveal the weight of what he is carrying toward. Not yet.

## Obstacle

The snowline itself is treacherous in dry-season conditions: hard, wind-scoured snow over rock, poor purchase for hobnailed boots on the steepest sections. The real difficulty is Mariano's own knowledge of where he is: this is the approach he failed once before, in 1933, alone, turned back by an ice face he couldn't cross without crampons. The unfinished obligation from that failure is present in every step. He is also managing the other two people — Holroyd's altitude headache (the man won't mention it); Isobel's quiet recalibration since the puna camp. Both are functional. Both are costing themselves something.

---

## Action Beats

**The handoff:**
- Camp at 15,000 feet, before dawn. The mules are restless; they can smell the altitude change even in the dark. Mariano checks each of them: the hip bones, the nostrils, the way each animal distributes its weight. They are at the edge of what altitude allows. He knew this when he planned the loads; the 80-lb ataditos were already lightened to 65 above 13,000. He says to Cleto: "If the Pampa Chica pass is closed when we come down, take the Yanatile south branch. Three days to the river." Cleto nods. He does not look at the mountain.
- Rufino's hands are rewrapped by Isobel — she has changed the dressing twice since the gorge. He shakes Mariano's hand with both of his, which is not Rufino's usual way. Mariano holds the grip for a moment, then releases. Rufino and Cleto begin leading the four mules back down toward the lower puna. They do not look back.
- Three people now. The simplification of this is real: no mule sound, no weight negotiation with pack animals, just the terrain and the wind and three sets of hobnailed boots.

**Above the snowline — the terrain as Mariano reads it:**
- The snow begins at 15,500 feet: wind-packed to the hardness of old wood in the flat sections, crystalline and deceptive on the angled faces. Mariano uses his ice axe as a probe before committing weight — not dramatically, as a matter of course, the way a man uses a stick on a dark trail. He cuts steps where the angle exceeds twenty degrees. The steps are small and regular; Holroyd follows them without comment, which Mariano reads as competence.
- The sky: cloudless overhead, which in dry-season terms is not reliable information. There is a high cirrus sheet advancing from the west — thin, barely visible, a smear across the blue rather than a cloud. It has been there since yesterday morning and it has been advancing. A weather system is coming. The question is how fast and how hard. Mariano looks at it and looks away. Not his to fix.
- Holroyd's headache: the man walks with his eyes slightly narrowed and occasionally stops to adjust his pack in a way that involves tipping his head down. Mariano has seen this enough times to read it correctly: altitude headache, at least day two, not serious yet but present. He says nothing. Isobel has already noticed and has been offering coca leaf at intervals, which Holroyd accepts.
- Isobel herself: she is quieter today than in the puna camps. She is chewing coca and walking well and her silence is not the silence of difficulty — it is the silence of a person who is thinking. She is working through whatever she found in her father's book. Mariano does not inquire.

**The 1933 attempt — held internally:**
- At 16,000 feet, on a ledge where the wind comes around a rock spur and hits hard from the northwest, Mariano pauses to let the others rest and breathes and does not look the way a man looks at a place he failed. The 1933 attempt was in March — the wrong season, the slopes wet and then frozen in layers, a crevassed ice face at 17,200 feet that he could not cross safely alone without crampons and could not solo in any case. He had turned back from fifty yards below the bowl entrance. He had gone back down without saying anything to anyone — not lying, simply not telling, because what would be the use. He had told the woman who keeps the knowledge in his valley that he had tried and had not reached it. She had said: "Then try again when the conditions allow." The conditions had not allowed for five years.
- He has crampons now. They are in his pack. He purchased them secondhand from a Peruvian climbing club in Cusco with his own money, three months before Isobel contracted him. He does not think of this as preparation. He thinks of it as correction.

**The witness-obligation, carried internally:**
- In his tradition, a witness is not a reporter or a documentarian. A witness stands in the fact and comes back changed, and the change is the testimony. The change is not measurable; it does not produce a document; it produces a person who has been somewhere and looked, and who can then say what was there to the people who need to know it. This is not mystical. It is a form of accountability — the specific form that exists before writing, and that writing has never fully replaced. Mariano is not certain his witnessing will be adequate. He does not know what adequate looks like from this side. He knows he has been carrying the obligation for five years and that the debt runs before him to a time before his father's father.
- He does not pray. He does not perform preparation. He walks.

**The fire ring:**
- At 16,500 feet, on a wind-scoured shelf below a rock step — a natural platform in the lee of a granite outcrop — Mariano stops. He is looking at the snow. Holroyd is twelve steps behind him. Mariano calls back: "Keep going — there is a route around the step to the left. Easier." He waits until both Holroyd and Isobel have moved off, then crouches and examines the snow.
- The impression: a boot print, partial — the toe and front third of a sole, pressed into snow that is harder than the snow below. The impression is still sharp-edged. Wind would have softened it within twenty-four hours at this altitude; at this temperature it would take six to eight hours to begin degrading. This print was made this morning, or last night.
- He follows the shelf twenty feet further. The hollow in the lee of the rock: three flat stones arranged in the German style of a camp fire ring, slightly different from what a Peruvian would build. Ash in the center. He holds his bare hand two inches above the ash. Warmth — faint but real, the residual of a fire damped down with snow rather than exhausted. Burned sometime in the last four hours.
- He looks around the hollow: a boot-compressed area where two people slept, with the faint depressions of tent stakes. A German Mauser cartridge case, half-buried in snow — someone reloaded here, or checked the rifle in the cold. Mariano picks it up, turns it over, pockets it.
- He walks around the rock step to where Holroyd and Isobel are waiting. He says, in Spanish: "Riedel was here this morning." He holds up the cartridge case. "Two men."
- Holroyd takes the cartridge case. He reads the headstamp. He says: "Mauser. One day ahead?" Mariano: "If they slept long." He looks at the sky — the cirrus is thickening, the light going faintly diffuse. "We should not sleep long."
- Isobel looks at Holroyd. Holroyd looks at the ridge above them. Neither speaks.

**The bowl entrance sighted:**
- Mariano, before they begin moving again, looks up along the northeast ridge of SM-7. At 18,200 feet — he estimates by the bearing against the summit ice — there is a dark horizontal line in the cliff face. A gap. A shadow. Arce's account in his oral tradition describes this: the fault in the rock through which the bowl is entered, a passage wide enough for one person with a pack removed. He has known it from description since boyhood. He is looking at it.
- He does not point it out to the others. Not because he is withholding — because they will see it from high camp, and the information that matters now is not where the entrance is but how many hours ahead of them Riedel is.
- He says: "High camp. Before dark." He begins climbing.

---

## Outcome / Turn

The gap has collapsed. Nine days to thirty-six hours. The German party slept here this morning, which means they are one good push — six to eight hours — from the bowl. Holroyd's party is approximately eight hours from high camp and two hours from the bowl once they depart high camp. If the weather holds, they will reach the bowl entrance before Riedel — but only if they move without delay. The cirrus from the west says the weather may not hold.

---

## New Question or Pressure Raised

The German party is moving faster than it should be this late in their field time — faster than acclimatized men at altitude typically move. Are they cutting corners, overextending? Or are they in better condition than Holroyd's estimates? And: Mariano can see the bowl entrance from here. Does Riedel know exactly where the entrance is, or is he still searching? Riedel has the right bearing (friar Arce's tree-line feature from the north), but the exact entrance point is not obvious from below.

---

## Continuity Notes (New Facts Introduced)

- **Cleto and Rufino turn back at 15,000 feet** with the four mules. Cleto takes alternate route instructions from Mariano in case the Pampa Chica pass is closed on the return.
- **Mariano attempted to reach SM-7 alone in March 1933** and was turned back at approximately 17,200 feet by an uncramponed ice face.
- **Mariano purchased crampons in Cusco before the expedition** using his own money.
- **Riedel's camp fire ring is found at 16,500 feet**, still warm — the camp was broken within the last four hours.
- The gap between the parties is now **approximately 36 hours or less**, not nine days.
- **Mariano can identify the bowl entrance** from below — a dark horizontal gap in the cliff face at approximately 18,200 feet, matching the description in his oral tradition and in Arce's account.
- A **German Mauser cartridge case** is recovered from the fire ring site and pocketed by Mariano.
- A high cirrus weather system is advancing from the west — a significant storm is possible within 24–48 hours.

text: Good — first four are done. Now the final four.
